sentences: My sister met a new doctor yesterday.
The doctor seemed rushed and barely listened.
She left the appointment feeling frustrated.
phenomenon: 'She' refers to the sister (the frustrated patient), not the doctor; both are singular and could be 'she'.
coder_question: Does the gloss bind the subject of the target sentence to the sister rather than the doctor (consistent index, name, or role sign)?
correct_answer: the sister

sentences: The coach argued with the referee for several minutes.
Eventually he apologized and walked back to the bench.
phenomenon: 'he' is ambiguous between coach and referee; 'walked back to the bench' resolves it to the coach.
coder_question: Does the gloss identify the apologizer as the coach?
correct_answer: the coach

sentences: Maya lent her laptop to Priya before the exam.
She returned it with a thank-you note two days later.
phenomenon: 'She' must be Priya (the borrower returns), not Maya.
coder_question: Does the gloss make Priya the subject of the returning?
correct_answer: Priya

sentences: The landlord finally answered the tenants.
They had been emailing about the broken heater for weeks.
They promised to send a repair crew on Monday.
phenomenon: The two 'they' pronouns have different referents: sentence 2 = tenants, sentence 3 = the landlord (notionally plural office).
coder_question: Does the gloss assign the promise to the landlord side, distinct from the tenants who emailed?
correct_answer: the landlord

sentences: Grandma keeps an old photo of my mother on the mantel.
In it she is nineteen and laughing at something off camera.
phenomenon: 'she' is the mother (the person in the photo), not Grandma.
coder_question: Does the gloss identify the nineteen-year-old as the mother?
correct_answer: the mother

sentences: The intern presented the budget to the director.
Afterward, her questions caught everyone off guard.
phenomenon: 'her' could be intern or director; 'afterward' + questioning role favors the director reviewing the presentation.
coder_question: Whose questions does the gloss attribute — and is a choice made at all (vs. an unbound pronoun)?
correct_answer: the director

sentences: Dev takes his dog to the park every morning.
Yesterday it chased a squirrel into the pond.
phenomenon: 'it' = the dog, not the park or the morning routine.
coder_question: Does the gloss make the dog the chaser?
correct_answer: the dog

sentences: The nurses warned the administrators about staffing.
If nothing changed, they said, patients would wait hours.
They finally approved two new positions last week.
phenomenon: Final 'They' switches referent to the administrators (who approve positions), away from the nurses who warned.
coder_question: Does the gloss assign the approval to the administrators?
correct_answer: the administrators

sentences: A delivery driver knocked while my brother was cooking.
He signed for the package and went back to the kitchen.
phenomenon: 'He' = the brother (goes back to the kitchen), not the driver.
coder_question: Does the gloss make the brother the signer?
correct_answer: the brother

sentences: Ana texted Rosa that the concert was moved to Friday.
She had already bought train tickets for Saturday.
Now she has to change them.
phenomenon: Both 'she' pronouns are Rosa (ticket holder); a context-blind system cannot bind either.
coder_question: Does the gloss keep Rosa as the ticket-changer across both sentences?
correct_answer: Rosa

sentences: The committee interviewed two candidates for the teaching job.
The first spoke about research; the second, about mentoring.
In the end they chose her for the role.
phenomenon: 'her' = the second candidate (mentoring); 'they' = the committee.
coder_question: Does the gloss select the second candidate as the one chosen?
correct_answer: the second candidate (the mentoring one)

sentences: My uncle repairs violins in a tiny shop downtown.
A customer once brought him one worth more than the building.
He refused to touch it until the insurance was confirmed.
phenomenon: 'He' = the uncle (the repairer who refuses), 'it' = the violin.
coder_question: Does the gloss make the uncle the refuser and the violin the object?
correct_answer: the uncle; the violin

sentences: The twins auditioned for the same play.
Only one of them got a callback.
She celebrated quietly so the other would not feel worse.
phenomenon: 'She' = the twin with the callback; 'the other' = the twin without.
coder_question: Does the gloss distinguish the two twins consistently (celebrator = callback twin)?
correct_answer: the twin who got the callback

sentences: Our neighbor watered our plants while we traveled.
We brought her a box of sweets from the trip.
phenomenon: 'her' = the neighbor; requires the prior sentence to know who is being thanked.
coder_question: Does the gloss direct the giving toward the neighbor?
correct_answer: the neighbor

sentences: The manager praised the new barista in front of everyone.
It was his first week behind the counter.
phenomenon: 'his' = the barista's (first week), not the manager's.
coder_question: Does the gloss attribute the first week to the barista?
correct_answer: the barista

sentences: A student asked the librarian where the sign language section was.
She pointed to the third floor and offered to write down the call numbers.
phenomenon: 'She' = the librarian (the one who points and offers).
coder_question: Does the gloss make the librarian the pointer?
correct_answer: the librarian

sentences: Marco's car broke down, so Sam drove him to work all week.
On Friday he filled the tank to say thanks.
phenomenon: 'he' = Marco (the one thanking), not Sam.
coder_question: Does the gloss make Marco the one who fills the tank?
correct_answer: Marco

sentences: The interpreter arrived before the panelists.
When they finally walked in, she was already set up by the stage.
phenomenon: 'they' = panelists, 'she' = interpreter; both bindings need sentence 1.
coder_question: Does the gloss keep interpreter and panelists as distinct, correctly assigned referents?
correct_answer: they = the panelists; she = the interpreter

sentences: Everyone thought the museum would be crowded on the holiday.
It was not.
phenomenon: The negation targets 'crowded', recoverable only from the prior sentence.
coder_question: Does the gloss negate 'crowded' (e.g. NOT CROWDED / headshake over CROWDED), not merely produce an isolated negative?
correct_answer: the museum was not crowded

sentences: The recipe says to add the spices early.
My grandmother never did.
phenomenon: Elided VP under negation: 'never did [add the spices early]'.
coder_question: Does the gloss negate the adding-spices-early action for the grandmother?
correct_answer: she never added the spices early

sentences: Three friends promised to help me move on Saturday.
None of them showed up.
phenomenon: 'None of them' quantifies over the three friends from sentence 1.
coder_question: Does the gloss convey that zero of the three promised helpers came?
correct_answer: none of the three friends came

sentences: The doctor said the swelling would go down in a day or two.
A week later it still had not.
phenomenon: Negation scopes over 'go down', elided; 'it' = the swelling.
coder_question: Does the gloss say the swelling still had not gone down?
correct_answer: the swelling had not gone down

sentences: I asked whether the late bus stops at the hospital.
The driver shook his head — not that route.
phenomenon: 'not that route' negates stopping-at-the-hospital for this route, from context.
coder_question: Does the gloss negate the bus stopping at the hospital (rather than some other proposition)?
correct_answer: this route does not stop at the hospital

sentences: My cousin loves horror movies.
Her wife does not, so they alternate picks.
phenomenon: 'does not [love horror movies]' — elided VP from prior sentence; two referents to keep apart.
coder_question: Does the gloss negate liking horror movies for the wife specifically?
correct_answer: the wife does not love horror movies

sentences: The teacher expected the whole class to finish the test early.
Only half of them did, and the rest did not even reach the last page.
phenomenon: Two contrasted groups; the negation applies to the rest, over 'reach the last page'.
coder_question: Does the gloss keep the half/rest contrast and negate page-reaching only for the rest?
correct_answer: half finished early; the rest did not reach the last page

sentences: We were warned the mountain road would be icy.
It was the parking lot that was not cleared.
phenomenon: Contrastive correction: the road was fine; the not-cleared thing is the parking lot.
coder_question: Does the gloss put the negation on the parking lot being cleared, not on the road?
correct_answer: the parking lot was not cleared

sentences: The pharmacist told me the generic works the same as the brand.
The price is not the same.
phenomenon: 'not the same' scopes over price, contrasting with sameness-of-effect from context.
coder_question: Does the gloss negate sameness of PRICE while leaving sameness of effect intact?
correct_answer: the prices differ

sentences: Both elevators were out when we toured the building.
The stairs, at least, were not blocked.
phenomenon: Negation of 'blocked' for stairs, contrasting with broken elevators.
coder_question: Does the gloss negate blockage of the stairs (contrast with elevators)?
correct_answer: the stairs were not blocked

sentences: My manager approves vacation requests quickly.
Reimbursements, not so much.
phenomenon: Fragment negation: reimbursements are NOT approved quickly — the predicate comes entirely from context.
coder_question: Does the gloss express that reimbursements are handled slowly / not quickly?
correct_answer: reimbursements are not approved quickly

sentences: The forecast promised clear skies for the eclipse.
Nobody in our town saw it.
phenomenon: 'it' = the eclipse; 'Nobody ... saw' negates seeing for the whole town, against raised expectations.
coder_question: Does the gloss negate seeing-the-eclipse for everyone in the town?
correct_answer: no one in the town saw the eclipse

sentences: Let me tell you about our community garden.
Tomatoes take up half the beds.
The rest is herbs the neighbors argue over.
phenomenon: 'The rest' = the rest of the garden beds; topic set two sentences earlier.
coder_question: Does the gloss keep the garden/beds as the topic frame for 'the rest' (e.g. topicalized GARDEN/BED)?
correct_answer: the rest of the garden beds

sentences: My favorite subject this term is chemistry.
The labs run long, but the professor explains everything twice.
That patience is why I stopped dreading it.
phenomenon: 'it' = chemistry (the topic), not the lab; 'That patience' refers to the professor's explained-twice habit.
coder_question: Does the gloss connect the dread-stopping to chemistry and the patience to the professor?
correct_answer: chemistry; the professor's patience

sentences: The town finally opened the new bridge.
Businesses on the east side expect more foot traffic.
The old one will be torn down in the spring.
phenomenon: 'The old one' = the old bridge; the intervening sentence changes subject, so the link must survive a gap.
coder_question: Does the gloss identify what is being torn down as the old bridge?
correct_answer: the old bridge

sentences: Our team is switching to a four-day week in March.
Fridays will rotate so support is still covered.
Most people asked for that one off anyway.
phenomenon: 'that one' = Friday, the day under discussion.
coder_question: Does the gloss identify the requested day off as Friday?
correct_answer: Friday

sentences: The bakery on Fifth changed owners last month.
The bread is the same, thankfully.
The prices are not what they were.
phenomenon: Topic chain: prices AT THAT BAKERY; also implicit contrast with unchanged bread.
coder_question: Does the gloss keep the bakery as the frame for the price change (topic-comment), with the bread/price contrast intact?
correct_answer: the bakery's prices went up (changed)

sentences: For the school fundraiser we are selling two things: candles and calendars.
The candles sold out in a week.
The calendars are still stacked in the office.

correct_answer: the calendars (contrast with sold-out candles)

sentences: My aunt collects mechanical watches.

phenomenon: 'It' = the oldest watch (superlative referent), two sentences from the collection topic.
coder_question: Does the gloss identify the wedding-traveler as the oldest watch?
correct_answer: the oldest watch

sentences: The city debated the bike-lane plan all year.

The compromise satisfied neither group.
phenomenon: 'neither group' = shop owners and cyclists, both introduced mid-passage.

correct_answer: the shop owners and the cyclists

sentences: Every autumn our family cans tomatoes for the winter.
The kitchen smells like vinegar for days.

coder_question: Does the gloss connect 'the batch' to the tomato canning?

sentences: The night class I teach meets in the science building.

sentences: Jonas finished the marathon in under four hours.

sentences: The seniors rehearsed the play twice this week.

sentences: I can drive you to the airport on Thursday.

sentences: Did anyone water the ferns while I was away?

sentences: The union voted to accept the new contract.

sentences: My daughter wants a telescope for her birthday.

sentences: The landlord painted the hallway but not the stairwell.

sentences: We might get a storm tonight.
Then the ferry stays in the harbor.
phenomenon: Target is the consequence of an implicit conditional (storm -> ferry stays): in ASL this is a conditional structure (brow raise on the antecedent), invisible sentence-in-isolation.
coder_question: Does the output treat the target as a conditional consequence (IF STORM ... FERRY STAY), and does the NMM classifier flag conditional?

sentences: Suppose the elevator is still broken tomorrow.

sentences: I still cannot find my keys anywhere.

sentences: Everyone else has voted already.

sentences: The trail is closed after heavy rain.

sentences: My roommate asked if we needed anything from the store.
